title: OAuth2 (Backend)
objectives: Distinguish OAuth2 roles and grant types
Choose the right flow for a client type

sections.markdown: OAuth 2.0 is an authorization delegation framework. The user grants a third-party client scoped access to their resources without sharing their password. **OpenID Connect (OIDC)** layers identity on top with an ID token.

rows: TVs, CLIs, IoT with limited input

sections.markdown: The Implicit and Password grants are **deprecated**. Modern answer: 'Authorization Code + PKCE for user-facing apps, Client Credentials for machine-to-machine.' PKCE prevents authorization-code interception on public clients.
Storing client_secret in a mobile/SPA (public client) — attackers can extract it. Public clients use PKCE with no secret; only confidential (server) clients hold secrets.
Use a mature identity provider (Auth0, Okta, Keycloak, Cognito). Validate ID tokens with JWKS. Request minimal scopes. Rotate refresh tokens and detect reuse (evidence of theft).

flashcards.back: Resource Owner (user), Client (app), Authorization Server (issues tokens), Resource Server (your API).
OIDC adds identity: an ID token that says who the user is.

quiz.q: The recommended flow for a mobile app is…
quiz.explain: PKCE protects the code exchange on public clients.
No user is involved — the client acts on its own behalf.

options: An ID token identifying the user
quiz.explain: OIDC layers identity on OAuth2 via a signed ID token.

challenges.text: Configure a Resource Server that accepts JWTs from your IdP.
Add 'Sign in with Google' using Authorization Code + PKCE.
Build a service-to-service call using Client Credentials + audience-scoped tokens.
Implement refresh-token rotation with reuse detection.

interviewQuestions: Which grant is default for mobile/SPA and why?
How do you detect stolen refresh tokens?
